title: Oil Surges 16% on the Week as Gulf Strikes Spread, Fed Hawks Break Ranks, Trump Rattles China Truce
subhead: Brent closes above $88 and posts its biggest weekly gain since the war began as US-Iran strikes enter a seventh night and tanker mines close Hormuz, while a second Fed official warns of rate hikes and Trump's China voter-data accusations unsettle the trade truce.

sections.heading: Oil Posts 16% Weekly Gain as Conflict Expands to Kuwait and Bahrain
sections.body:
Brent crude settled at $88.10 a barrel on Friday, up 4.59% on the day and roughly 16% for the week — its largest weekly advance since the Iran war began. WTI closed at $82.49, up 4.48% on the day. Both benchmarks hit their highest levels since mid-June.

The week's escalation was geographic as well as military. Iran struck a power and desalination plant in Kuwait, its first direct attack on the emirate's civilian infrastructure, according to AsiaOne. Qatar's defence ministry said it thwarted an Iranian missile attack early on Friday, with a child wounded by interceptor shrapnel. Air raid sirens sounded across Bahrain three times in a single morning, News18 reported, with residents urged to seek shelter. Kuwait's military said its air defences were responding to a new Iranian drone and missile attack.

Iran's Revolutionary Guard Corps claimed two oil tankers exploded after hitting mines south of the Strait of Hormuz, and separately said its forces halted four US-protected ships attempting to transit the strait. CENTCOM dismissed the tanker mine claims. IRGC official Mohsen Rezaei warned Iran could launch a 'full-scale offensive' if US strikes continue, saying Tehran would no longer limit itself to proportional responses.

Saudi Arabia has diverted more than 70% of its daily crude exports to the Red Sea port of Yanbu, with shipments averaging 4 million barrels per day — up from 973,000 bpd in the same period last year, according to PVM Oil Associates analyst Tamas Varga. Andrew Lipow of Lipow Oil Associates warned: 'If more tankers come under fire and become damaged, we are going to see oil prices continue to move up as shipowners simply refuse to enter the Persian Gulf.'
sections.whyItMatters: The conflict has expanded from a bilateral US-Iran confrontation into a regional crisis hitting Kuwait, Qatar, and Bahrain — all US allies hosting major military bases. The IRGC's mine claims, whether verified or not, are already functioning as a deterrent: tanker operators will price in the risk, and insurance premiums will rise. Saudi Arabia's Yanbu diversion at 4 million bpd is a record, but it still leaves the market short of the pre-war Hormuz throughput of roughly 20% of global supply.

sections.heading: Chevron and ConocoPhillips Sign $60 Billion Iraq Pipeline Deals
sections.body:
US oil companies have signed roughly $60 billion in agreements and partnerships with the Iraqi government, including deals to develop alternative shipping routes that bypass the Strait of Hormuz, the Washington Post reported.

Chevron signed memorandums of understanding to explore a pipeline corridor across Iraq, while ConocoPhillips advanced separate agreements tied to the same objective, according to Al-Monitor. The deals were announced during Iraqi Prime Minister Ali al-Zaidi's visit to the White House this week.

Iraq and Syria also signed an agreement to restore an oil pipeline that would provide an alternative route for crude exports away from Hormuz, multiple outlets reported. However, CNBC analysis warned that existing pipelines cannot eliminate the vulnerability Iran poses to Middle East crude exports: capacity constraints, security risks along overland routes, and the sheer volume that still requires tanker shipping mean infrastructure bypasses are partial solutions at best.

ABC Australia reported that Gulf nations have been building alternatives to the Strait of Hormuz for years, but the current crisis has made the pace and scale of those projects unprecedented. The UAE's existing Habshan-Fujairah pipeline can move about 1.8 million bpd — a fraction of what transits Hormuz.
sections.whyItMatters: The $60 billion figure signals that US oil majors and the Iraqi government are treating the Hormuz closure as semi-permanent, not temporary. But the critical constraint is time: pipelines take years to build, and the CNBC analysis confirms existing infrastructure covers only a fraction of disrupted flows. The Syria pipeline agreement adds a geopolitical dimension — it requires Assad-era infrastructure and Russian-protected airspace, creating dependencies that complicate every future negotiation.

sections.heading: Second Fed Official Warns Rate Hikes May Be Needed as Oil Reverses Inflation Decline
sections.body:
Cleveland Fed President Beth Hammack joined the chorus of Federal Reserve officials warning that interest rate increases could be necessary, the Washington Post reported. Hammack's comments follow similar hawkish signals from other Fed policymakers, most notably Governor Kevin Warsh, who has drawn a line at 2% inflation.

Market pricing via CME Group Fed funds futures still shows just a 10% probability of a July rate hike, but the probability of a 25bps increase by September has risen to 48%. The shift reflects a growing recognition that June's soft CPI data — which showed the largest monthly drop in four years — was a ceasefire-era artifact that will be fully reversed by the energy price surge of the past two weeks.

Washington Post reported that Trump is publicly pressing for rate cuts, creating a public clash with his own appointees. The disconnect between the White House's demand for easier policy and the inflation data that the war is generating puts the Fed in an increasingly uncomfortable position.

US retail sales for June rose just 0.2%, down from 1% in May, with gasoline spending falling 5.3% — a figure that will look very different in the July data now that fuel prices have surged.
sections.whyItMatters: The Fed now has two problems: an inflation impulse from energy prices that will hit the July and August CPI prints, and a White House that wants the opposite policy response. Hammack's hawkish turn matters because she was not previously seen as a rate-hike advocate — it signals the hawkish wing is widening. If September CPI confirms the energy-driven reversal, a rate hike becomes the base case, not the tail risk.

sections.heading: Trump Accuses China of Stealing 220 Million US Voter Files, Rattling Trade Truce
sections.body:
President Trump used a primetime national address on July 16 to accuse China of pulling off 'the largest compromise of election data in history,' alleging Beijing acquired 220 million US voter files dating back to the 2020 election cycle, CryptoBriefing and NBC News reported.

Whether or not the allegation holds up to scrutiny, markets reacted swiftly. Bitcoin dropped below $63,000 on July 17 as investors hit the risk-off button, with the China allegations compounding anxiety over the US-Iran escalation.

NBC News reported that newly declassified documents released by the Trump administration offered scant evidence for the claims. Republican Senators John Cornyn and Bill Cassidy publicly criticized the speech, with Cornyn — a Senate Intelligence Committee member — saying 'there wasn't a whole lot new' and Cassidy warning it could set up 'a Democratic wave election' in the midterms.

The accusations threaten the US-China trade truce that has been one of the few stabilizing elements in global markets during the Iran crisis. Brazil also entered the tariff fray this week, calling Trump's 25% tariff 'unjustifiable' and threatening reciprocal measures, LiveMint reported.
sections.whyItMatters: The China voter-data allegations are a wild card that markets have not fully priced. If the claims lead to new China tariffs or sanctions, they would add a second front to a global trade landscape already strained by the Senate's 100% Russia-oil tariff bill. The bipartisan Senate pushback, however, suggests the allegations may not translate into immediate legislative action — but executive orders do not require Senate votes.

sections.heading: Japan June Core CPI Seen at 1.6%, BOJ Set to Hold as Government Backs Off
sections.body:
Japan's nationwide core CPI is expected to rise 1.6% year-on-year in June, up from 1.4% in May, according to a Reuters poll of 16 economists reported by EconoTimes and FreeMalaysiaToday. The pickup reflects higher energy costs linked to the Middle East conflict and revised medical service fees — but inflation would remain below the BOJ's 2% target for a fifth consecutive month.

Japan's wholesale inflation climbed 7.1% in June, marking its fastest annual increase since March 2023 and highlighting persistent cost pressures for businesses that will eventually feed through to consumer prices.

The Mainichi Shimbun reported that the Japanese government's final draft of its economic blueprint specifies that monetary policy steps will be left to the BOJ, after an earlier version was interpreted as pressure against further rate hikes. The clarification removes a potential obstacle to BOJ action if inflation data support it.

Mizuho Securities economist Ryosuke Katagi said the economic impact of the Iran conflict 'is likely to become more evident during the summer as energy costs continue filtering through the economy.' The official June CPI report is scheduled for release on July 24, ahead of the BOJ's policy meeting later this month.
sections.whyItMatters: The government's clarification that monetary policy is the BOJ's call is significant — it removes political cover for inaction. With wholesale inflation at 7.1% and core CPI trending back toward 2%, the BOJ has a data-driven case for a rate hike. Whether it acts at the late-July meeting or waits depends on how strongly the energy pass-through shows up in the July CPI print — but the institutional friction that was slowing BOJ action has been reduced.

sections.heading: IMF Praises UAE Resilience as Gulf Credit Spreads Widen
sections.body:
An IMF staff visit concluded on July 17 with a statement confirming the UAE economy's 'exceptional resilience' to the regional conflict, TradeArabia and Mirage News reported. The IMF highlighted the UAE's proactive readiness and continued financial sector growth, despite the country shifting from stagnation to a 0.5% contraction in the latest Reuters poll.

The endorsement matters because the UAE is positioning itself as a safe-haven economy within the Gulf — a contrast to Kuwait and Qatar, which face 8.1% contractions. The UAE's diversified economy, dollar peg, and financial centre status give it buffers that pure hydrocarbon exporters lack.

However, credit markets are pricing the risk. Bahrain's five-year CDS climbed 6bps to 285bps, and Qatar's rose 1bp to 33bps, per S&P Global Market Intelligence. The spread between Bahrain and Qatar CDS — a proxy for Gulf credit stress — has widened significantly since the conflict began.

The IMF also noted that global disinflation has stalled, a signal that the Iran conflict's energy price effects are feeding through worldwide inflation metrics and complicating the central bank easing cycle that many had expected for the second half of 2026.
sections.whyItMatters: The IMF's UAE endorsement is a vote of confidence, but the credit markets tell a different story for the rest of the Gulf. The 285bps CDS on Bahrain reflects genuine default risk — a sovereign in an 5.1% contraction with limited fiscal buffers. The IMF's broader point about stalled global disinflation confirms what the Fed hawks are saying: the Iran war has broken the inflation-decline narrative that was supposed to define H2 2026.

sections.heading: Senate Russia Sanctions Bill Advances With 60 Co-Sponsors
sections.body:
The bipartisan Sanctioning Russia Act of 2026 now has approximately 60 Senate co-sponsors, according to The Hindu and Financial Express, significantly expanding from the 26 co-sponsors reported earlier this month. The bill proposes tariffs of up to 100% on imports from countries that continue purchasing Russian crude oil — primarily India, China, Slovakia, Hungary, and Azerbaijan.

India's government said it is 'monitoring' the proposed legislation but has not outlined specific countermeasures. A senior Indian official told The Hindu that the bill reflects 'double standards' given European exemptions for natural gas imports from Russia.

The bill includes presidential waiver authority allowing the White House to suspend tariffs on individual countries. Energy experts have warned that removing Russian crude from global markets would tighten an already strained supply picture — potentially adding another $10-15 per barrel to prices at a time when Hormuz disruption has already removed significant volume.

The legislative momentum comes as the Senate also blocked additional war spending earlier this month, creating an inconsistent signal: Congress wants to punish Russia's customers but has been reluctant to fund the military campaign that is driving oil markets.
sections.whyItMatters: The jump from 26 to roughly 60 co-sponsors takes the bill from a fringe initiative to a credible legislative threat. India and China will now have to plan for a scenario where 100% tariffs on their exports to the US become real — which could reshape global trade flows far beyond the oil market. The inconsistency with war-spending blockage is the telling detail: the Senate is willing to weaponize trade, but not to fund the conflict that is creating the energy crisis in the first place.

bottomLine: Oil's 16% weekly surge is the market pricing in a regional escalation that no longer stops at Iran's borders — Kuwait, Qatar, and Bahrain are now directly affected, and the IRGC is mining tanker lanes. The Chevron-ConocoPhillips $60 billion Iraq pipeline deals signal that major oil companies treat the Hormuz disruption as semi-permanent, even though existing bypasses cover only a fraction of lost throughput. On the monetary policy front, Beth Hammack's hawkish turn widens the Fed's rate-hike coalition, while Japan's government has removed the political obstacle to BOJ action. Trump's China voter-data allegations and the Senate Russia sanctions bill at 60 co-sponsors add two separate trade-war vectors that could compound the energy-driven inflation shock. The common thread across all these stories: the assumption that the Hormuz disruption and its inflationary effects are temporary is losing credibility by the day.
sources: AsiaOne, News18, Anadolu Agency, CNBC, ABC Australia, Washington Post, Al-Monitor, CryptoBriefing, NBC News, LiveMint, EconoTimes, FreeMalaysiaToday, Mainichi Shimbun, Reuters (via poll), TradeArabia, Mirage News, S&P Global Market Intelligence, The Hindu, Financial Express, CME Group, Lipow Oil Associates, PVM Oil Associates, Mizuho Securities, S&P Global Market Intelligence
